posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
sorry if this isn’t concise, it seems clear in my head - but when typing it up it looks jarbled.

I am not commenting in the 2019 vintage itself, but it’s impact on how we form opinions on domaines turning the corner/new producers deserving praise.

I’ve been trying a bunch of 2019 Red Burg in the past 6-7 months from random producers. It seems a vintage that has juicy early drinking wines from all ranges. Especially producers that typically aren’t known for making clear fruit driven wines. I can’t remember the last bottle of 2019 I’ve been disappointed by, from Bourgogne up to the top level wine.

But my concern is this

I see people state that “X” domaine has turned a corner based on a 2019 sampling. Or check out “Y” upstart domaine, they just came out and the 2019 is AMAZING. But most importantly this makes me wonder, is 2019 one of those vintages that just gave the vast majority of the producers the ability to make at the minimum decent wine on release that they wouldn’t/haven’t been able to do in other vintages? Will be be disappointed when buying the 2020 (maybe not 2020) and 2021 of the same producers without the proven track records that seemed to turn it on/around in 2019?

or in sum -

Are we just seeing a flash in the plan for many producers or can we extrapolate this top vintage to producers turning the corner or evidence that a new domaine will be excellent?

posts.name: M. Ch@o
posts.cooked: Not sure how you could ever generalize about a producer based on a single vintage. Doesn’t the fact that 2019 has generally turned out well across the board suggest that you shouldn’t use it generalize about a producer going forward? Absent knowing about real viticultural, harvest, elevage, etc. changes, which can only suggest potential changes in quality, think you would need a number of additional vintages to draw any conclusions.

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked:
“Especially producers that typically aren’t known for making clear fruit driven wines.”

I think the important word you used is ‘clear’. When you use that word, do you mean “clearly fruit driven wines” (i.e., a more forward vintage) or “clear, fruit driven” where “clear” refers to [insert your descriptor here]? I ask because “fruit driven” would - I assume - be easily explained by the vintage characteristics (warm year, more fruit, higher alcohols?). But “clear” would not be something I would associate with vintage weather, but winemaker style?

Regardless, happy to hear such positive notes on 2019! I am deep the vintage.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
Very good thought. 2019’s across the board have so much early appeal right now that when someone says I just tried (fill in the blank) _______ 2019 and it was amazing, I’m taking it with a grain of salt. Not that I don’t believe them that the wine is delicious right now but that it’s delicious in the way that so many other 2019s are. So I agree that it’s not like X producer knocked it out of the park this vintage any more than so many others did.

With some producers I could already see it in the comparison of 2018 to 2019. With some the gap in the two vintages isn’t all that big, with others it’s huge. And yeah I think we’ll see with some of these hot new producers whether they can keep up the quality in 2020 and 2021 when those come out.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked: I think you make a very good point Charlie. Just about every 2019 I’ve had has been like this, and I’ve never seen anything like this previously in other warm years, like 1990 and 2003…not only juicy and early drinking but also fairly fresh and (relatively) balanced. I have bought a few things out of sheer momentum, and with some surprise that pricing on a few things i like hasn’t been as terrible as projected. But I really have no feel for what these will turn out like in 20 or 30 years.

thats an interesting thought for sure. I do know I’ve read a few comments on producers where 2019 was their breakout year or something similar.

I gotta say though, I’m not super upset about a year where the minimum wine made is still pretty solid. the right plan for me is still to lean more heavily on producers I trust, and work on building that trust in the newer ones as they accumulate good vintages.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
i think you’re getting at this: yes, there will be tons of wines from producers in 2019 that are outliers for those domaines because of the vintage. because of this, the statement that a domaine has “turned the corner” in 2019 is inherently premature given the nature of the vintage; i don’t know how many vintages it takes to show a domaine is doing well, but it’s more than 1.

i think we should all be thankful that there’s a lot of 2019s, and that the quality - especially at the entry / lower levels - tends to be astounding.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: I don’t get the trickery angle. Every time there’s a top vintage you see excellent wines coming from unlikely places, that’s what makes them top vintages.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I don’t think 2019 is quite that good of a vintage, so I guess I don’t feel particularly tricked by it? I’ve had 2019s I’ve disliked as too ripe or unbalanced. I have a couple of friends who actually don’t like 2019 generally and who I think will like 2020 (and especially 2022) better.

To me, the one bellwether vintage is 2010 - almost everyone made good wines that year, and good winemakers made great wines. I don’t think 2019 is in that category.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
I see 2019 not as a trick but as an invitation to try a few more new producers than I otherwise might. Which I did. Looking through my notes I’m a bit surprised how many I’ve already opened. But that was my plan! Most long term friends did well, and many of my newer sipping partners delivered pleasing wines. I’ll likely try many of them in future vintages before going too deep.

Here’s a very brief summary. Everything was either Bourgogne or village level, where I usually do a lot of my early tasting.

Consistent buys: H. Lignier, Fourrier, Bertheau, Hudelot Noellat. No disappointments, and I bought a good chunk of all of these.

Less experience: J. Voillot. I’ve like these wines in several vintages, and bought a few back vintages to try those too. The 2019s showed very well.

New (to me): Berthaut-Gerbet, Marchand-Tawse, Lumpp, Amiot-Servelle, F. Esmonin, Guillemot. All except the Lumpp (which I found a bit riper than I like) showed well, with a lot of variety and experession of terroir. I could imagine buying nearly any of these in future vintages, and likely will.

A rising tide vintage to be sure, but I can’t think of how that’s a bad thing.

posts.name: M1chael Dom1ngo
posts.cooked:
CFu:

I agree. But I feel like I’m seeing a lot of commentary that

X Domaine turned a corner

Or

Upstart Y domaine is making incredible wines

Based on 2019

I think it’s really about how you want to contextualize those X or Y statements because right now they are just predictive. They could be true for 2019 with the full context later being:

X Domaine that turned the corner in 2019 regressed to their mean in 2020 or 2021

Or

Upstart Y domaine made incredible wines in 2019 and couldn’t fulfill on that promise in 2020

All that to say it’s fun to try and predict a domain’s future/potential off of a vintage but we’ll never know till that future comes to pass. I guess it’s about the datapoints behind the wine like “why was Domaine X able to turn that corner beyond just good vintage”?

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked: Plus 1, or count me in the group that feels 2019 is an easy to like vintage(lots of fruit) but not so much why I love Burgundy.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I’ve been trying a bunch of 2019 Red Burg in the past 6-7 months from random producers. It seems a vintage that has juicy early drinking wines from all ranges. Especially producers that typically aren’t known for making clear fruit driven wines. I can’t remember the last bottle of 2019 I’ve been disappointed by, from Bourgogne up to the top level wine.

I am not buying that much better Burgundy anymore and so have not had that many 2019s. But, your comments made me think of the way I first felt drinking 1999s. When the 1999s first came out, I had already tasted many young Burgundy vintages, but none as good as 1999. As I said, I have not had much 2019s, but sometimes there just is a vintage that is better than other vintages. Some that I have seen like this over the years have been 1982 in Bordeaux (and maybe 2016), 1990 and 2019 in Germany, 1999 and 2010 in red Burgundy, 2014 in white Burgundy, etc. In each of these instances, there we producers that made wines much better than they generally did. And, in many (but not all) of these instances there were people discussing how the wines would not age well because they tasted too good, too young (they were wrong).

posts.name: Christian A Conner
posts.cooked:
image1170×1533 186 KB

image1170×1971 136 KB

image1170×1474 157 KB

2019 DRC VR1er tasted August 17 - stunning, life-affirming, like chewing on Zeus’ armpit

2019 Bouchard Volnay Caillerets 1er tasted August 19 - took an hour or so to show itself but once it did it was quite a bruiser

2019 Duband GC village tasted August 15 - simply superb, also took awhile to reveal itself

No tricks with any of these, all I see is fabulosity. It seems to me like mediocre and legendary producers alike making good lemonade out of good lemons is a good thing.

What does that say about 2019 vs any forthcoming vintage? My only answer would be, if you can afford to try more of the ones you liked, try more. If you can’t, find what you can afford based on your previous experience. If that sounds too simplistic, forgive me. The joy and heartbreak is all part of what makes burgundy so special in my humble opinion. Consistent producers will stay consistent. New buds emerge. Don’t get too wrapped up in missing out, your wine miracle will come! You can always rely on Didier-Fornerol!!

I would also say it’s much more interesting to talk about specific producers instead of speaking in X’s and Y’s. As I’m sure we all have learned, Burgundy is a tiny place that contains a universe.

posts.name: Fred Bower
posts.cooked:
This thread was the trick. I fell for it. Opened a de Montille Volnay Taillepieds last night. Fuchsia color with medium transparency. Initially shy…it took about an hour to get going. Aromas all in red fruit spectrum with focus on cherry, raspberry, and cranberry most prevalent. Florals in the violet and rose range emerge with some air. Palate also took time to unfold but ultimately started showing off a nice array of tart cherry, cranberry, black tea, raspberry, and eventually some cinnamon spice.Finish is medium at this point. I expect this to flesh out and develop with time. I have no good comparison points with the old guard vs. new guard at de Montille, but my intuition is that this needs another decade to go through that development.

Cheers,
fred

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
As someone who made a couple of barrels in 2019, as well as tasted them extensively from barrel and bottle, I can definitely say that it was the sort of vintage that made winemakers feel intelligent. It was very hard not to make wines that didn’t at least have a lot of appeal… And many producers made great wines.

However, I am not really seeing that a “never been better” narrative has been generalized throughout the region. Some emerging or improving producers certainly did especially well in 2019, of course, but I am struggling to think of many people who made stunning 19s and then went on to totally miss 2020.

posts.name: Fred Daniels
posts.cooked:
I think there are a lot of “issues” with the Burg market today…trying to extrapolate from 2019 is probably not going to work reliably for the reason that in this vintage, maybe like 2016 or some others, anyone with grape juice could make a tasty wine. The real turnaround domaines would be hidden in with all those “one hit” producers and really no way to tell. Like trying to judge how someone will do on a test by whether they spelled their name correctly at the top…perfect on the easy questions but how will they do with the actual test?

That said, it is a great vintage to find values and possible gems, but I must protest the stupid pricing that is going into the stratosphere for everything. The dollar is stronger than it has been in years yet the quality of the 2019s has been used as an excuse to overcharge relentlessly. And it’s not just DRC…and the wines are snapped up. Brave new world…

posts.name: Christian A Conner
posts.cooked: Surprised to hear 2016 as opposed to, say, 2014. I would argue that the ‘test’ of a winemaker happens every year - on this thread one could mistake winemaking for the way president of a country gets to take credit for economic boons that happen under their watch regardless of their administration’s policies, Bill Clinton could say he stewarded a great economy because the tech bubble was in full swing under his watch, Bush Jr.’s administration saw the housing market implode with disastrous consequences, and on and on. I think it’s funny that there’s a reluctance to see good wine as good wine as if it were a fluke. Improvisation and good fortune are what separates the Hannibals from the Washingtons, one might have all the right moves but still lose the war and vice versa.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: 2021 is going to be a great test! The best 2021s are really beautiful. Hyper perfumed, melting tannins, low alcohol, not much in the way of weight but very good persistence. The perfect antidote to the big years we have been having. Despite the trauma of the growing season I would actually sign up to make the vintage again, if I could, though I doubt it would win me many friends.

thoughts on a couple of producers that have been mentioned here since my last visit to this thread:

J Voillot: at the risk of this getting more popular, this is a consistent buy for me. I really like these wines almost every year. 19 is not a one-off for these

Souyard: I typically like these wines. I think the white is a pretty awesome value normally, the reds are good but at the base level, I’d still rather drink Chanterêves base levels for sure. but they do tend to please crowds

posts.name: Nick Amis
posts.cooked: I felt the same way, but having tasted some Tawse 2019s and 2020s I’d say the wines are now highly competent (and may turn out better than that) - glad to have bought a few from various levels from both vintages. Neal and Jasper have reviewed favourably too.

posts.name: Fred R
posts.cooked:
Sorry, late to the party, but we just tasted 14 2019 reds over the last 3 weeks and 1 white. Village/LD/ lower end PC for me so no I won’t and can’t comment on DRC

Very high success ratio, I think it’s a good year with some balance. We bought a bunch. Good value in lower end wines especially in Beaune/PC. But I see a lot here complaining 2019 is a warm year, but I was told it’s the coolest since maybe 2016?? What I did notice was a spread of alcohol, and the least successful for us were any wines with high alcohol (14%) but you can get them down to 12.5% just as well, the ratings went up with the decrease. It’s not just the label (which often lies) but even blind those ones tasted “hot”.

So any comments are appreciated on how warm/cool 2019 is relative to the current climate (last 10-20 years) not relative to 30-50 years it doesn’t help me my TARDIS is in the shop.

Said another way, I’m concerned the longevity of these wines but if they drink well early so what I am always short on ready to drink reds.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Nick_Amis:

I felt the same way, but having tasted some Tawse 2019s and 2020s I’d say the wines are now highly competent (and may turn out better than that) - glad to have bought a few from various levels from both vintages. Neal and Jasper have reviewed favourably too.

that’s kinda the point of my thread. Are the wines highly competent now? Or are the vintages just so good they take over whatever winemaking was in place for prior years?

Will they still be good in 21/22.

posts.name: Vlad B3rk0vsky
posts.cooked: I’m wondering how Camus handled the 2019 vintage…

posts.name: Fred R
posts.cooked:
CFu:

hat’s kinda the point of my thread. Are the wines highly competent now? Or are the vintages just so good they take over whatever winemaking was in place for prior years?

Will they still be good in 21/22.

I am far from expert in Burgundy … I taste, I like, I buy, if I have space. Simple.

That said, I generally prefer cooler more classic wines. I tried to check 20/21/22 weather reports and it seems there is nothing great there. Some other whispers I heard were “buy the cool the vintages” meaning with climate change there are less and less cool ones. My conclusion (correct or incorrect) was stick to 2016 and 2019. That is what I have done. I will wait for 2023 to see if it picks up. You can still get reasonable 2016s, they are not common but if you look you can scrounge some, so I figure I’ll be able to backfill these two vintages until the next one.

Of course if tastings open up more I will continue to taste 20/21 for fun, but unless I’m blown away I will stick to what I know.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Fred_R0me_r0:

What I did notice was a spread of alcohol, and the least successful for us were any wines with high alcohol (14%) but you can get them down to 12.5% just as well, the ratings went up with the decrease.

Interesting. I mean, a warm year or warm site doesn’t force you to pick at higher brix. (Note that some well regarded PN sites in CA are quite hot, but have the right soil and are managed that, so are picked in balance.) Here, your sampling points towards choices. That optimal ripeness came at a lower brix, and some chose to disregard that or failed to recognize it. At least, to your preferences.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Fred_R0me_r0:
CFu:

hat’s kinda the point of my thread. Are the wines highly competent now? Or are the vintages just so good they take over whatever winemaking was in place for prior years?

Will they still be good in 21/22.

I am far from expert in Burgundy … I taste, I like, I buy, if I have space. Simple.

That said, I generally prefer cooler more classic wines. I tried to check 20/21/22 weather reports and it seems there is nothing great there. Some other whispers I heard were “buy the cool the vintages” meaning with climate change there are less and less cool ones. My conclusion (correct or incorrect) was stick to 2016 and 2019. That is what I have done. I will wait for 2023 to see if it picks up. You can still get reasonable 2016s, they are not common but if you look you can scrounge some, so I figure I’ll be able to backfill these two vintages until the next one.

Of course if tastings open up more I will continue to taste 20/21 for fun, but unless I’m blown away I will stick to what I know.

Without making any value judgments, 2021 is a significantly cooler year that will see drastically different wines than any of 2018, 2019, 2020 or 2022. I would also not say 2019 is a significantly “cooler” vintage than 2018 or 2020. From a temperature perspective they’re quite similar, even if they generally produced pretty different wines. Finally, I’d say 2017 is cooler than 2016.

In general, I don’t think average temperature is necessarily a great handicap for the style of a vintage.
